topic: Two people discover they've been working on identical projects
raw_output:
[story 1]

The shared drive was the first flicker of unease. Sarah stared at the screen, her knuckles white where she gripped the mouse. The code—the complex algorithm they had both spent the last three weeks perfecting—was exactly the same. A chilling certainty settled in her stomach, a cold knot tightening with every line of identical syntax. She glanced up, the fluorescent lights of the office seeming too bright, too accusatory. Was this a coincidence? Or was someone, or something, deliberately mirroring her efforts? She minimized the window, then immediately reopened it, needing to verify, needing to see if the duplication was real. Her breath hitched slightly as she typed the next command, the silence of the cubicle farm amplifying the frantic drumming in her ears.

[story 2]

Mark kept wiping his palms on his jeans, a nervous habit he hadn't realized he’d developed. He leaned back in his chair, the cheap plastic groaning under his weight, and looked across the desk at Chloe. Her face was calm, almost serene, as she reviewed the final iteration of the prototype. The specifications matched his notes perfectly, down to the obscure variable names. A sudden, sharp intake of breath escaped him. He felt a distinct prickling sensation along his scalp, as if he were being watched from every corner of the room. He quickly shuffled his papers, the rustle of the edges sounding deafeningly loud. He needed proof, something external to validate the unsettling feeling that he was utterly, profoundly duplicated.

[story 3]

"You finished the integration?" Alex asked, his voice deliberately level, though his eyes darted around the room, cataloging exits and shadows. Maya nodded, a little too quickly. She felt a strange, hollow echo inside her chest, a sensation of being observed by an unseen audience. When she pulled up the shared repository, the file structure was flawless, the documentation identical to the version she had just committed. Her fingers hovered over the keyboard, trembling just enough that she had to consciously steady them. She leaned in close to the monitor, her jaw tight, trying to discern any subtle difference—a misplaced comma, a different timestamp—but there was nothing. Only perfect, terrifying symmetry.
